DAM Capital notes that “while component plans were delayed, it remains on track to start delivering meaningfully in FY28E, implying near-term margin pressures (baked in our est at 3.2 per cent – 60bps decline yoy).”New growth avenues beyond smartphonesLooking beyond the core mobile segment, the brokerage sees multiple optionalities opening up for Dixon. It points out a “strong pick up in IT/telecom and potential entry into EMS and servers/optical transceivers” as additional engines of growth over the medium term. These newer verticals are expected to diversify revenue streams and reduce reliance on the highly competitive smartphone assembly business.Strong earnings outlook remains intactDespite short-term headwinds, the brokerage remains confident about Dixon’s earnings trajectory. It expects the company to deliver robust growth metrics, stating that “Dixon delivering its component plans with gradual scale up in volumes… should drive 50 per cent EPS CAGR over FY26–28E (excl one-time gain in FY26), over 30 per cent RoCE, and healthy FCF.”Dixon currently trades at around 40 times FY28 earnings, but DAM Capital believes there is scope for further re-rating. The report notes that “higher certainty of Vivo JV, PLI 2.0 and scale up ex-mobiles along with inorganic acqns for PCBA will act as key positive triggers for re-rating.”(Disclaimer: The above article is meant for informational purposes only, and should not be considered as any investment advice. ET NOW DIGITAL suggests its readers/audience to consult their financial advisors before making any money related decisions.)

title: Bank Holiday Today: SBI, HDFC Bank, ICICI Bank, Axis Bank branches shut in multiple cities on Muharram; UPI, ATM services continue
description: Bank Holiday on June 26: Bank branches in several Indian cities are closed today for Muharram, while digital banking, ATMs, NEFT, IMPS, and RTGS remain active. Stock markets are also expected to remain closed.
content: Bank Holiday Today: Banking operations across several Indian cities are affected today (June 26) as branches remain closed on account of Muharram, marking the Islamic New Year and the observance of Ashura, one of the most significant days in the Islamic calendar.According to the Reserve Bank of India holiday calendar, bank branches will remain shut in select locations, while digital banking services continue without disruption.Cities where bank branches are closed todayBanking services at physical branches are not available in the following cities:Agartala, Aizawl, Belapur, Bengaluru, Bhopal, Chennai, Hyderabad, Jammu, Kanpur, Kolkata, Lucknow, Mumbai, Nagpur, New Delhi, Patna, Raipur, Ranchi, Srinagar, and Vijayawada.Banking services remain operational digitallyDespite branch closures, core banking operations remain accessible. Customers can continue using internet banking, mobile banking apps, Unified Payments Interface (UPI), and ATMs for withdrawals, transfers, and bill payments.Routine branch-based services such as cash deposits, account opening, loan applications, passbook updates, and large-value over-the-counter transactions are unavailable during the holiday.However, digital rails, including NEFT, IMPS, and RTGS remain operational for online transactions, though branch-initiated requests will follow holiday schedules.Cities where banks remain openIn contrast, banking operations continue as usual in several other cities, including Ahmedabad, Bhubaneswar, Chandigarh, Dehradun, Guwahati, Gangtok, Imphal, Itanagar, Jaipur, Kochi, Kohima, Panaji, Shillong, Shimla, and Thiruvananthapuram.However, readers should note that bank holidays in India are determined by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) based on national, regional, and festival calendars. Muharram observance typically results in selective closures across states, rather than a nationwide shutdown of banking services.Digital banking infrastructure continues to ensure uninterrupted financial access even during physical branch closures.Stock Market Holiday TodayEquity markets in India are expected to remain closed today. The National Stock Exchange (NSE) and the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE) will remain closed for three days as June 26 falls on a Friday, followed by the regular weekend. Trading will resume on Monday, June 29.There will be no trading in the equity, equity derivatives, currency derivatives, Securities Lending and Borrowing (SLB), Electronic Gold Receipts (EGR), NDS-RST and Tri-Party Repo segments during the holiday. While the Multi-Commodity Exchange (MCX), however, will observe only a partial trading holiday and will resume in the evening session from 5:00 pm until 11:30 pm or 11:55 pm.Read More: NSE, BSE closed on June 26 for Muharram; equity markets set for 3-day suspension

title: Oil edges up from $70; Brent holds near $75 amid West Asia tensions
description: Oil Price: Brent crude was trading at USD 75.01 a barrel, down marginally by USD 0.25 or 0.33 per cent, while U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate crude stood at USD 71.61, slipping USD 0.31 or 0.43 per cent in choppy trade.
content: Oil prices edged higher on Friday, with Brent crude hovering near the USD 75 per barrel mark, as geopolitical developments in West Asia added a layer of uncertainty to already mixed global market signals.Brent crude was trading at USD 75.01 a barrel, down marginally by USD 0.25 or 0.33 per cent, while U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate crude stood at USD 71.61, slipping USD 0.31 or 0.43 per cent in choppy trade. Despite the slight declines in benchmark prices, the broader tone in the market remained supported by rising geopolitical risk premiums.The latest trigger came from escalating rhetoric around the Strait of Hormuz, a critical artery for global oil shipments. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps has reportedly rejected a newly proposed shipping route through the strait, arguing that it was established without Tehran’s approval. The IRGC warned that vessels must adhere to corridors designated by Iran, raising concerns about potential enforcement action against ships that fail to comply.The Strait of Hormuz handles roughly a fifth of the world’s oil trade, and any disruption or perceived threat to navigation tends to reverberate quickly across energy markets. U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio struck a firm tone on Iran, stating that the country continues to be led by what he described as “radical clerics.” Rubio indicated that Washington retains “multiple options” if diplomatic talks with Tehran fail, underscoring the fragile nature of ongoing negotiations.Rubio also dismissed discussions around implementing tolls or fees for passage through the Strait of Hormuz, calling such proposals unworkable. Fees and tolls are effectively the same thing, he said, reiterating that the U.S. would not support any arrangement that could hinder maritime flow or undermine Gulf allies. He added that recent engagements with Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) nations were constructive and reaffirmed regional cooperation.While geopolitical tensions offered some support to crude prices, broader financial market signals remained mixed. On Wall Street, the Dow Jones Industrial Average climbed to fresh record highs, buoyed by gains in industrial and financial stocks. However, the tech-heavy Nasdaq index faced renewed selling pressure, reflecting investor caution around high-growth sectors.Oil Price HistoryOil prices have remained highly volatile, with concerns mounting over potential supply disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime chokepoint that handles nearly 20 per cent of global oil flows, heightening inflation risks for economies worldwide.Earlier on April 30, Brent climbed near a multi-year high of USD 126 a barrel, gaining nearly 7 percent intraday, extending the momentum built on the back of a strong surge earlier. Before, on March 9, Brent crude surged over 27 per cent to trade at a multi-year high of USD 119 a barrel amid escalating West Asia tensions.(Disclaimer: The above article is meant for informational purposes only, and should not be considered as any investment advice. ET NOW DIGITAL suggests its readers/audience to consult their financial advisors before making any money related decisions.)

title: Women's T20 World Cup 2026: India-W return to winning ways with a 5-wicket win over Bangladesh-W
description: India-W attained a 5-wicket win over Bangladesh-W in Women's T20 World Cup 2026 on Thursday. Shafali Verma emerged as the standout performer for India.
content: After enduring a painful defeat against South Africa on Sunday, India-W have returned to winning ways in fine fashion, beating Bangladesh-W by 5 wickets at Old Trafford on Thursday in the Women's T20 World Cup 2026. Batting first, Ban-W posted a par total of 136 for 8. In reply, Ind-W chased down the total inside 17 overs. Shafali Verma emerged as the standout performer for India with a 34-ball 53.Shafali Verma bludgeoned the Bangladesh attack with a 34-ball 53 to set up a five-wicket win for India in the Women’s T20 World Cup Thursday, notwithstanding another poor fielding effort from her team.India’s abysmal fielding in tournament thus far hit a new low as Bangladesh managed to post 136 for eight at Old Trafford.India, who lost to South Africa largely due to their poor fielding, dropped as many as four catches in the powerplay.The target was very gettable and Shafali provided a flying start to ensure that India got home in 16.5 overs. There was bit of lull in the middle overs before Jemimah Rodrigues (26 off 15 balls) took India close to the finishing line.Having lost to South Africa, India will need to beat Australia June 28 in their bid to make the semifinals.Smriti Mandhana fell cheaply but Shafali single-handedly fired India to 63 for one in the powerplay. She punished the spinners and pacers with equal disdain, unleashing the inside-out shot on multiple occasions.She brought her second World Cup fifty off 29 balls in the eighth over, taking India on the cusp of a big win.The explosive opener was stumped in a rather unusual fashion as she went for a non-existent single after under edging a ball from Nahida Akter in the ninth over, not realising the wicketkeeper had collected the ball and dislodged the stumps.Earlier, all the four dropped catches were rather straightforward but the fielders made a mess of them. Among the guilty were Nandni Sharma, Yastika Bhatia and Radha Yadav, who had grassed two chances in the loss to South Africa in the previous game.The biggest beneficiary of India’s indiscretion was Juairiya Ferdous, who was dropped thrice by the fifth over. Ferdous did hit some fine boundaries but failed to make the most of the luck that came her way.After being warned twice for running on the pitch, Nandni, who had dropped Ferdous and Sobhana Mostary off successive balls, took a sharp return catch to get rid of Ferdous. The opener scored 33 off 31 balls.India have been found wanting in all departments in the tournament thus far. The management has been guilty of making too many changes in the pace department, leaving out the likes of Kranti Gaud.On Thursday, Renuka Thakur got her first game of the competition and she struck in her very first over. Opener Dilara Akter (4) went for the pull but could not clear Shree Charani at deep square leg.Charani bowled a brilliant 20th over, picking up two wickets and giving away only three runs.Unlike her fielding, Radha (3/28) had a better outing with the ball as she removed opposition captain Nigar Sultana (32 off 27 balls) and Mostary (22 off 26 balls) in quick succession.Considering the unusual heat in the UK, spinners have been getting more assistance. India too predominantly stuck to spin in Bangladesh’s innings.Besides the dropped catches, India bowled seven wides and three no-balls.(With agency inputs)

title: Stock Market Holiday Today: NSE, BSE closed on June 26 for Muharram; equity markets set for 3 day suspension
description: Stock Market Holiday on June 26: Indian stock markets remained closed on June 26 for Muharram, with trading suspended across the NSE and BSE segments. Commodity trading on MCX resumes in the evening, while equity markets reopen on June 29.
content: Stock Market Holiday Today: Indian stock markets will remain closed on Friday on the occasion of Muharram, halting trading across equity, derivatives and currency segments on the National Stock Exchange (NSE) and the Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE), according to the exchanges' holiday calendar.The closure gives investors a three-day market break as June 26 falls on a Friday, followed by the regular weekend. Trading will resume on Monday, June 29.There will be no trading in the equity, equity derivatives, currency derivatives, Securities Lending and Borrowing (SLB), Electronic Gold Receipts (EGR), NDS-RST and Tri-Party Repo segments during the holiday.MCX to reopen for evening sessionThe Multi-Commodity Exchange (MCX), however, will observe only a partial trading holiday. Commodity trading will remain suspended during the morning session from 9:00 am to 5:00 pm and resume in the evening session from 5:00 pm until 11:30 pm or 11:55 pm, depending on the commodity contract.Upcoming holidays for BSE, NSEMuharram marks one of the scheduled exchange holidays for 2026. Indian stock exchanges have notified 16 market holidays for the year, with several already observed.The next full market holiday is scheduled for September 14 on account of Ganesh Chaturthi. Trading will also remain suspended later in the year for Gandhi Jayanti on October 2, Dussehra on October 20, Diwali Balipratipada on November 10, Guru Nanak Jayanti on November 24 and Christmas on December 25.NSE, BSE HolidayDateGanesh ChaturthiSeptember 14Gandhi JayantiOctober 2DussehraOctober 20Diwali BalipratipadaNovember 10Guru Nanak JayantiNovember 24ChristmasDecember 25Four other holidays, including Mahashivratri, Eid-Ul-Fitr, Independence Day and Diwali Laxmi Pujan, fall on weekends in 2026 and therefore do not affect trading schedules.Stock Market on June 25Indian equities ended marginally higher on Thursday ahead of the holiday, supported by gains in automobile and banking stocks. The BSE Sensex closed 109 points, or 0.14 per cent, higher at 77,100.47, while the NSE Nifty 50 rose 0.14 per cent to end near 24,056.IndiGo, Mahindra & Mahindra and Maruti Suzuki led the gains among Sensex constituents, while banking stocks, including ICICI Bank and State Bank of India (SBI),  also contributed to the benchmarks' advance.Also Read: Gold Price Today: Precious metal holds above USD 4,000 but faces 7th straight weekly loss - What's driving the downfall?

title: Gold Price Today: Precious metal holds above USD 4,000 but faces 7th straight weekly loss - What's driving the downfall?
description: Gold traded near USD 4,023 after recovering above USD 4,000 but remained set for a seventh straight weekly loss amid a stronger dollar and Fed rate hike expectations. Silver hovered near USD 58, down nearly 11% this week.
content: Gold Price Today: Gold prices traded nearly flat on Friday after rebounding above the USD 4,000 per ounce mark in the previous session. However, the precious metal remains on track to post its seventh consecutive weekly decline, weighed down by a stronger US dollar and persistent expectations of a Federal Reserve interest rate hike.Gold Price TodayAs of 5:10 AM, spot gold was trading nearly unchanged at USD 4,023 per ounce. During Thursday's early trading session, bullion slipped below the key USD 4,000 level before recovering after the release of the latest US Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE) inflation data, which matched market expectations. Despite the rebound, gold has declined more than 3 per cent this week, marking its longest weekly losing streak in recent months.Silver Price TodaySilver prices also traded in a narrow range, hovering around USD 58 per ounce. The white metal has remained under heavy selling pressure in recent weeks and is on track to record a weekly decline of nearly 11 per cent, significantly underperforming gold.MCX Gold and Silver Trading TodayTrading in precious metals on the Multi-Commodity Exchange (MCX) will remain suspended during the first half of Friday's session due to the Moharram holiday.In the previous trading session:MCX Gold settled at Rs 1,43,200 per 10 grams.MCX Silver ended 0.6 per cent higher at Rs 2,20,940 per kg.Why Are Gold Prices Falling?The ongoing weakness in gold prices is largely driven by the strength of the US Dollar. The US Dollar Index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of six major currencies, has gained nearly 0.7 per cent this week and touched a 52-week high of 101.80.A stronger dollar typically makes gold more expensive for overseas buyers, reducing demand for the precious metal and putting downward pressure on prices.However, the dollar eased slightly after Thursday's PCE inflation report came in line with expectations, reducing immediate concerns over higher inflation.Fed Rate Hike ExpectationsFollowing the inflation data, expectations for a Federal Reserve interest rate hike at the December FOMC meeting eased marginally. According to the CME FedWatch Tool, the probability of a rate hike slipped from 83 per cent to around 81 per cent, offering temporary support to gold prices.Oil Prices Add Pressure to GoldCrude oil prices strengthened on Friday after the United Nations' International Maritime Organisation (IMO) suspended a vessel evacuation plan following an attack on a Singapore-flagged ship in the Strait of Hormuz.As a result:Brent crude futures climbed back above USD 75 per barrel.WTI crude traded above USD 71 per barrel.Higher oil prices can fuel inflation, reinforcing expectations of tighter monetary policy by the Federal Reserve. Elevated interest rates generally reduce the appeal of non-yielding assets like gold, adding further pressure on bullion prices.Gold Price OutlookGold continues to face headwinds from a resilient US dollar, elevated Treasury yields, and expectations of tighter monetary policy. Investors will closely monitor upcoming US economic data and comments from Federal Reserve officials for further direction. Meanwhile, movements in the dollar and crude oil prices are likely to remain key drivers for gold and silver in the near term.(Disclaimer: The above article is meant for informational purposes only, and should not be considered as any investment advice. ET NOW DIGITAL suggests its readers/audience to consult their financial advisors before making any money-related decisions.)

title: Mirzapur: The Movie teaser has fans asking the same question: Why is Jitendra Kumar aka Jeetu Bhaiya playing Bablu Pandit?
description: The teaser of Mirzapur: The Movie has sparked a fresh online debate after fans noticed Jitendra Kumar stepping into the role of Bablu Pandit, earlier played by Vikrant Massey.
content: The teaser of Mirzapur: The Movie has done exactly what the makers would have wanted: it has got fans talking. Released on Thursday, the first glimpse of the film brought back some of the franchise’s biggest faces, including Pankaj Tripathi as Kaleen Bhaiya, Ali Fazal as Guddu Pandit and Divyenndu as Munna Bhaiya. But amid all the excitement over the return of familiar characters, one casting detail quickly became the centre of online discussion.Viewers noticed that Jitendra Kumar appears to be playing Bablu Pandit, the role originally played by Vikrant Massey in the web series. The reveal has triggered a wave of reactions online, with some fans curious about the change and others openly saying they miss Massey in the part.A teaser full of old favourites and one big surpriseThe teaser leans heavily into the dark, violent and swagger-filled world that made Mirzapur such a breakout hit. Kaleen Bhaiya is back with his trademark menace, Guddu looks set for another bloody power battle, and Munna Bhaiya’s return has already become one of the teaser’s biggest talking points.But it was Jitendra Kumar’s appearance in the Pandit family setup that caught many fans off guard. In the original series, Bablu Pandit was one of the most important emotional anchors of the story, and Vikrant Massey’s performance left a strong mark on viewers. So the moment audiences connected Jitendra Kumar to that role, social media lit up.Fans split over Jitendra Kumar replacing Vikrant MasseyThe reaction online has been mixed, though not entirely negative. Some viewers said Jitendra Kumar looked promising and were willing to see how the film handles the character. Others, however, said the role feels incomplete without Vikrant Massey.Several users on X said the teaser looked exciting overall, but added that Bablu Pandit is a character they still strongly associate with Massey. A number of posts described him as “missed” in the teaser, while some questioned why the makers chose to recast such a familiar role for the film adaptation.At the same time, a section of fans defended Jitendra Kumar, pointing out that he has already proved his range in projects far removed from Panchayat. For them, the casting change is unusual, but not necessarily a deal-breaker.Why Bablu Pandit matters so much to Mirzapur fansThe strong reaction is not surprising. Bablu Pandit was never just a supporting character in Mirzapur. He was central to the emotional arc of the first season and played a major role in shaping Guddu’s journey. His death remains one of the key turning points in the franchise.That is also why any actor stepping into the role was always going to face intense scrutiny. For a large section of the fanbase, Bablu is inseparable from Vikrant Massey’s performance, and that emotional attachment is clearly showing up in the response to the teaser.The film expands the Mirzapur worldThe movie version appears to be taking the Mirzapur universe to a larger, more cinematic scale. The teaser hints at bigger action, a broader canvas and a story that revisits some of the franchise’s most popular characters while also introducing fresh faces.Along with Tripathi, Fazal, Divyenndu and Jitendra Kumar, the film features a large ensemble that includes Abhishek Banerjee, Rasika Dugal, Shweta Tripathi, Shriya Pilgaonkar, Harshita Gaur, Sheeba Chadha, Rajesh Tailang, Pramod Pathak, Sonal Chauhan and Mohit Malik, among others.Release date locked for September 2026Mirzapur: The Movie is set to release in theatres on September 4, 2026, in Hindi and Telugu. With the teaser now out and fan reactions pouring in, the film has already kicked off the next round of conversation around one of India’s most popular streaming franchises.For now, one thing is clear: the teaser has revived the Mirzapur buzz in a big way. But alongside the excitement over Munna Bhaiya’s return and Kaleen Bhaiya’s presence, it is the Bablu Pandit casting switch that has become the internet’s first big debate around the film.

title: Germany vs Ecuador FIFA World Cup 2026: Kickoff time, TV channels and streaming platforms around the world
description: Ahead of the start of the FIFA World Cup 2026 game between Germany and Ecuador, let's take a look at how to watch the match live. Get hold of the details given.
content: Germany and Ecuador will lock horns in their respective final Group E game of the FIFA World Cup 2026. The match will take place at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey. Germany, the four-time champions, have already qualified for the Round of 32 stage. However, Ecuador are still hanging and would need a draw or a win to advance. With all to look forward to, let's see who comes out as the winner.Canada reject more than 70% of FIFA World Cup visa requests from India: Report Kick-off TimeIndia (IST): 1:30 AM Friday  USA (EDT - East Coast, e.g., New York): 4:00 PM Thursday  UK (BST): 9:00 PM Thursday  France (CEST): 10:00 PM Thursday  Germany (CEST): 10:00 PM Thursday  Brazil (BRT): 5:00 PM Thursday  Portugal (WEST): 9:00 PM Thursday  Argentina (ART): 5:00 PM Thursday  Spain (CEST): 10:00 PM Thursday  Australia (AEST, e.g., Sydney): 6:00 AM FridayFIFA World Cup 2026: Vinicius Junior scores twice as Brazil beat Scotland 3-0 to qualify for Round of 32 How to watch the live streaming of the Germany vs Ecuador FIFA World Cup 2026 game?IndiaIndian football fans can follow the FIFA World Cup 2026 through television and digital platforms. Fans can watch the live telecast of the match on the Unite8 Sport channel. Digital viewers can catch the live streaming on Zee5.USAAll matches are available in English on FOX Sports platforms, while Spanish-language coverage is provided by Telemundo and Universo. Streaming is available through the FOX Sports App, FOX One and Peacock.UKFootball fans in the United Kingdom can watch every World Cup match free-to-air on BBC and ITV, with live streaming available via BBC iPlayer and ITVX.AustraliaAustralian viewers can watch every match live on SBS and SBS Viceland, with streaming available on SBS On Demand.SpainIn Spain, World Cup coverage is available through RTVE and DAZN, with matches streamed via RTVE Play and the DAZN platform.BrazilBrazilian fans can tune in via TV Globo, SBT and CazéTV. Streaming options include Globoplay and CazéTV's digital platforms.ArgentinaCoverage in Argentina is provided by Telefe, TV Pública and TyC Sports, with online streaming available through their respective digital services.PortugalPortuguese viewers can follow the tournament through local sports broadcasters and streaming services, including RTP and associated digital platforms.FranceFrench coverage is led by M6 and beIN Sports. Fans can stream matches through 6play and beIN CONNECT.GermanyIn Germany, World Cup matches are available on ARD, ZDF and MagentaTV. Live streaming can be accessed through ARD Mediathek, ZDF's digital platform and MagentaTV.The Disgrace of Gijón: How the controversial 1982 Germany vs Austria World Cup match prompted FIFA to change rules of the game

title: IDFC First Bank's MD and CEO V Vaidyanathan stresses MSMEs must adopt AI, digitisation as Times Network kicks of ETNOW Leaders of Tomorrow Season 14
description: Launching Season 14 of ETNOW Leaders of Tomorrow, IDFC FIRST Bank's V. Vaidyanathan urged MSMEs to embrace AI and digitisation, highlighting India's demand-driven entrepreneurial advantage while addressing funding challenges for small businesses.
content: In an exclusive conversation with ETNOW Leaders of Tomorrow, V Vaidyanathan, MD & CEO, IDFC First Bank has warned India’s many MSME companies that “if we are not digitised and not using the full power of AI, we are going to miss big opportunities.” Kicking off the 14th season of ETNOW Leaders of Tomorrow, he also defined the India Advantage as demand, digitisation, entrepreneurship. “Some countries have demand but no digitisation. Some have digitisation but no demand. India has both— this is the right time and the right place,” he emphasised.One of India’s most committed voices on entrepreneurship and financial inclusion and with over 15 years of deep focus on the MSME segment, Vaidyanathan also captured the defining shift in India’s small business mindset, saying “Earlier people thought of business as a local business and operated in their zone. Now they’re thinking more national — some sectors are even going international.” From traditional dal mills and flour mills to digital-first D2C brands he argued the shift from offline to online is “a big, big deal” — and that AI is now the great equaliser: “Small companies with no staff can just do marketing on the fly.”But Vaidyanathan didn’t shy away from the hard truths either. On growth capital, he was blunt: “Out of 100 entrepreneurs, VCs invest in maybe 3 or 4. What about the balance 95? They don’t get equity — and without equity, you don’t get debt.” .His interview will air on ETNOW on Friday at 7.30 PM with a repeat telecast on Saturday at 6:30 PM.Times Network has launched the 14th season of its flagship entrepreneurship show, LEADERS OF TOMORROW, with the theme "Emerging Businesses: The India Advantage", highlighting the opportunities driving India's startup and MSME ecosystem. Presented by IDFC FIRST Bank and co-powered by STAR Health Insurance, the opening lineup for this season will feature conversations with Bharat Khera, Secretary, MSME, Government of India; Raghav Chandra, Co-Founder, Urban Company; CP Gurnani, Co-Founder, AIonOS; Sonu Sood; Abhinandan Lodha; Santosh Iyer, Managing Director and CEO, Mercedes-Benz India, Nobel Laureate Prof. Mohan Munasinghe and many more.While Leaders of Tomorrow Springboards will take the platform’s on-ground presence to 15 cities across India, LoT Vodcasts will continue to serve as a high-impact digital extension of the franchise, bringing audiences sharp, in-depth and candid conversations shaping India’s new-age entrepreneurial landscape. The season will culminate in March 2027 with the Leaders of Tomorrow Awards.

title: Anil Ambani moves NCLAT, challenges personal insolvency proceedings
description: Industrialist Anil Ambani has challenged the NCLT's personal insolvency order before NCLAT over SBI's Rs 853.25 crore claim. The appellate tribunal will hear the matter on July 10.
content: Industrialist Anil Ambani has approached the appellate tribunal NCLAT, filing an appeal against the initiation of personal insolvency against him by the NCLT over the petition filed by public sector lender SBI, PTI reported.The Mumbai bench of the National Company Law Tribunal had on June 11, admitted a plea filed by the State Bank of India, seeking personal insolvency against Ambani over default of loans by his group firms - Reliance Communications (RCOM) and Reliance Infratel Ltd (RITL).He was the personal guarantor of the loans extended to his group firms. Ambani's petition came for a hearing before a two-member bench comprising Justice Mohd Faiz Alam Khan and Barun Mitra. However, it was adjourned following the request from his lawyer, according to PTI."Having regard to the request made by Ld. Counsel for the appellant, list this matter on July 10, 2026, before the appropriate bench," said the NCLAT order dated June 24, 2026.SBI had moved an application under section 95 of the Insolvency & Bankruptcy Code before the NCLT, which allows creditors to start insolvency proceedings against debtors, including personal guarantors and partnership firms.The leading public sector lender had claimed a default of about Rs 853.25 crore as of March 1, 2019, arising from personal guarantees executed by Anil Ambani for facilities extended to RCOM and RITL.SBI had argued that despite approval of resolution plans for RCOM and RITL, its rights against the personal guarantor remained intact and enforceable, as per PTI.Moreover, Resolution Professional (RP) in his Section 99 report, concluded that debt and default were established and that the personal guarantee continued to remain enforceable.However, Ambani challenged the RP's report, arguing that the guarantee stood discharged after approval of the RCOM resolution plan. Moreover, he also alleged the report violated principles of natural justice.The NCLT rejected Ambani's argument that the guarantee was void because it was executed after the loan account had become an NPA, noting that the NPA classification was retrospective and followed a restructuring process.The tribunal in its order also noted SBI's contention that Ambani had filed multiple applications over the years, which delayed the progression of the insolvency proceedings, PTI report stated.Meanwhile, a spokesperson for Ambani said: "The order dated June 11 2026, passed by the NCLT, Mumbai, relates to a disputed personal guarantee allegedly extended by Ambani to the State Bank of India in 2016 - that is ten years ago - even before the promulgation of personal insolvency laws".The underlying facility had been availed by Reliance Communications for the repayment of its borrowings from Chinese lenders, and Ambani derived no personal benefit from the said funds, he added. (With Agency Inputs)

title: Canada reject more than 70% of FIFA World Cup visa requests from India: Report
description: More than 70 per cent of Indian nationals who applied for Canadian visitor visas to attend the FIFA World Cup 2026 got their applications rejected, according to data released by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC).
content: More than 70 per cent of Indian nationals who applied for Canadian visitor visas to attend the FIFA World Cup 2026 got their applications rejected, according to data released by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC).Data analysed by Canadian broadcaster CTV News revealed that only 355 of the 1225 visa applications submitted by Indians for the World Cup-related travel were accepted, which in other words can be reported as 29 per cent approval rate. The figures are from applications that were processed between November 15 and March 31.Indians were not the only ones who faced rejection as Canadian authorities processed nearly 17000 visitor visa applications linked to the World Cup during the period. Of these, only 41 per cent received approval, suggesting that applicants from several countries faced a challenging visa process.IDFC First Bank's CEO Vaidyanathan stresses MSMEs must adopt AI, digitisation as Times Network kicks of ETNOW Leaders of Tomorrow Season 14Among all countries, Ghana submitted the highest number of applications at 1725. However, fewer than 11 per cent of applications were granted visas. Columbia came second with 1630 applications and recorded one of the highest approval rates, with nearly 70 per cent of applicants receiving permission to travel.Pakistan were in the list as well, with 1250 applicants, and only 102 submissions from that part of the world got approved.Canada are one of the co-hosts of the ongoing iteration of the FIFA World Cup, alongside USA and Mexico. The country is scheduled to stage 13 matches across Toronto and Vancouver.Ahead of the start of the tournament, Canadian immigration authorities repeatedly warned travelers against misleading online claims about special visa arrangements for World Cup visitors.The IRCC has made it known that there is no dedicated "FIFA visa" or fast-track immigration route for fans travelling to the tournament. Visitors must follow the standard visa process or obtain an electronic travel authorisation, depending on their nationality.Authorities also cautioned people to be remain aware of immigration agents and consultants who claim they can guarantee visa approvals as a shortcut to settling in Canada.The warning comes amid concerns that some agents are exploiting the popularity of the World Cup by promoting misleading immigration claims, despite repeated government statements ruling out any special pathway for work permits or permanent residency through the tournament.Passport Fee Hike: Centre raises charges from July 1; fresh passport at Rs 2,500, tatkaal at Rs 5,000 - DETAILS

title: Centre lifts commercial LPG restrictions, restores bulk supply partly as West Asia pressure eases
description: The Centre has eased LPG supply restrictions imposed during the West Asia crisis, fully restoring commercial packed LPG supplies and partly resuming bulk LPG supply for industrial users.
content: In a relief for factories, hotels, restaurants and other commercial users of LPG, the Centre has eased restrictions on fuel supplies that were imposed during the West Asia crisis.The government on Thursday said it has removed all sector-wise restrictions on the supply of non-domestic packed LPG and restored commercial supplies to levels that existed before the crisis. It has also partly restored bulk LPG supplies, which had been suspended when the global supply situation worsened.The move is expected to ease pressure on industrial and commercial consumers who had faced supply curbs after the government diverted more fuel toward household cooking gas needs.Commercial LPG supplies restoredThe Petroleum Ministry said supplies of non-domestic packed LPG have now been fully restored to pre-crisis levels. This category is widely used by commercial establishments such as hotels, eateries, catering units and small businesses.Bulk LPG users have also received partial relief. The government has allowed supply of bulk LPG up to 50 per cent of pre-crisis consumption levels. Bulk LPG is used by a range of industrial and commercial consumers, including manufacturing units and large institutions. The restoration comes after the government reviewed domestic production and expected import availability.Why restrictions were imposed earlierThe curbs were introduced during the West Asia crisis, when concerns over global supply disruptions prompted the government to prioritise domestic LPG availability.At the time, the Centre used powers under the Essential Commodities Act to direct that C3 and C4 hydrocarbon streams be used only for LPG production. These streams are important feedstock for LPG, but they are also used by petrochemical and other downstream industries.By diverting them fully toward LPG production, the government ensured that household cooking gas supply remained stable even as global energy markets turned volatile.Government now allows more C3 and C4 for other industriesWith the supply situation improving, the government has now decided to reduce the diversion of C3 and C4 streams to the LPG pool.This means a larger share of these streams can once again go to petrochemical and other critical sectors. The Petroleum Ministry said the revised allocation will be done in a way that does not affect household LPG supply. It has also said total domestic LPG production will be kept at a minimum of 40 thousand metric tonnes per day.The Centre of High Technology has been asked to work out organisation-wise allocation of the enhanced C3 and C4 supply and send regular updates to the ministry.Domestic LPG supply remains the priorityThe ministry said the government’s first priority through the crisis was to ensure there was no disruption in cooking gas supply to households.According to the government, timely intervention and coordination among oil marketing companies helped maintain stable domestic LPG availability despite pressure on global supply chains.Even as restrictions on commercial supply are eased, the Centre has asked oil marketing companies to continue keeping detailed data on commercial and industrial LPG users. A common database across companies will also be maintained to improve planning and supply management.Push for PNG will continueThe government has also made it clear that its long-term policy on cleaner fuel use remains unchanged. Commercial and industrial consumers who have already shifted to piped natural gas, or PNG, will continue using PNG. Those who have access to PNG networks and are in the process of moving away from LPG will also be shifted gradually in coordination with city gas distribution companies.The Petroleum Secretary has written to chief secretaries of all states and Union Territories to ensure smooth implementation of the revised arrangement.What this means for businessesFor commercial and industrial consumers, the decision should bring immediate relief after months of uncertainty. Businesses that depend on LPG for day-to-day operations, especially those outside PNG networks, are likely to benefit from easier availability.At the same time, the government is trying to balance two competing goals: protecting household LPG supply and supporting industrial fuel demand as supply conditions improve.The easing of restrictions suggests that the Centre is now more confident about both domestic LPG production and import availability. But the gradual approach to bulk LPG restoration also shows that the government is still moving cautiously in a market shaped by global energy risks.

title: Tailored for all? Uneasy truths about marketing gender-fluid fashion
description: As a marketing scholar who's passionate about fashion, Maren Hoff noticed so many professional women wearing suit vests to work that she purchased a few herself.It may seem that gender norms are loosening, as women adopt male-sounding names and men wear nail polish. Indeed, fashion and workplaces seem to reflect a shift: During the past decade, retailers and brands such as Nordstrom, Marc Jacobs, and Gucci have launched gender-fluid collections, and some companies have relaxed dress codes to allow more gender-fluid expression at work.But the way these trends play out often reinforce, rather than upend, traditional power hierarchies, says Hoff, an assistant professor at Harvard Business School.That’s because uptake heads primarily in one direction: Women are more likely to adopt masculine styles than vice versa, likely because masculinity signals power and feels less risky than feminine markers. It may be easier as a woman to wear a vest than for a male colleague to come to work wearing dangly earrings, for instance.Hoff’s research, forthcoming in the Journal of Consumer Research, finds:During the past century, about 50% more traditionally male names in the US became gender-fluid in an average year than female names.Traditionally masculine style has influenced women’s apparel more than the inverse, according to an analysis of clothing sold by a large online retailer.“Despite this trend being there on the marketplace to serve inclusivity, it actually does perpetuate those power dynamics,” says Hoff, who wrote “The Asymmetry of Gender-Fluid Trends” with Silvia Bellezza, associate professor at Columbia Business School. “There's this power hierarchy that has traditionally existed, and in one way or another, still appears to exist.”Consumers blur traditional gender lines“Symbolic consumption”—choosing products and brands to express one’s identity or signal status—has long interested Hoff. “You can study these trends in names and learn something about how society thinks about certain tastes at any given point in time, and then that becomes relevant to marketing and fashion,” she says.Gender fluidity is a relatively recent phenomenon, says Hoff, but one gaining traction: A 2023 report found that 36% of US consumers have purchased clothing outside traditional gender categories. Hoff points to her survey of over 1,200 consumers in which respondents said a truly gender-fluid collection should look balanced—representing about a 50-50 split between traditionally male and traditionally female styles.The researchers began by analyzing how designers, brands, and popular culture define gender fluidity. By mining Google search results, they found that gender fluidity takes shape gradually over time and differs from gender bending (adopting names or products designed for the opposite sex), unisex (designed for both genders), and androgyny (combining elements of both genders in one name or product).“Many people associate gender-fluid fashion with a particular community [such as LGBTQIA+], but that’s not the case,” the paper explains. “Gender-fluid fashion is for everyone.”Also Read: Consumers are warming up to fluid fashion as gender stereotypes take backseatWhen names cross from boys to girlsThe researchers hypothesized that masculinity continues to “socially dominate” femininity both culturally and in commerce, despite efforts to reduce gender inequity. They conducted six studies: two large dataset analyses and several follow-up experiments.Girls' names are more likely to defy genderResearchers asked participants to rank how likely they would be to choose particular names for a baby girl or boy, scoring their answers from zero (least likely) to 15 (most likely).The authors analyzed the US Social Security records of the top 100,000 baby names between 1880 and 2022. They looked for names clearly associated with either men or women when they first appeared and charted whether and how the names transitioned to being used by the opposite sex over time.In 1883, five boys and no girls were named Blake. In the 1960s, the name started to become popular among girls, and in 1981, the team determined it had achieved crossover status. The researchers found:More male names crossed gender lines. On average, 310 male names per year became gender fluid.Fewer female names crossed over. By comparison, only 207 female names per year made the shift.Menswear influences women’s fashionThen, the researchers took their thesis to fashion, looking at how often an image-recognition model miscategorized menswear as womenswear and vice versa. Since the model is trained to recognize traditional gender norms, gender-fluid designs might confuse it, the researchers suspected.They scraped over 200,000 product images from global online retailer Farfetch and found that:7.1% of womenswear was mistaken as menswear5.3% of menswear was mistaken as womenswearWomenswear is more likely to be misclassifiedWhile a model accurately categorized most clothing images by gender, womenswear was more often misclassified (7.1%) than menswear (5.3%).Additional studies probed underlying motives. Ultimately, they found that women and non-binary people were less scared of negative reactions and more aware of male advantage than men. For that reason, they’re more likely to choose a gender-fluid product.In settings that challenge traditional hierarchies—like workplaces that prize feminine traits—men feel more comfortable adopting gender-fluid options.How to build awareness among business leadersWhy do women appear more open to gender fluidity? Hoff says it’s less risky—and potentially conveys an advantage—for women to send in a resume with a fluid name and to wear fluid outfits than it is for men.“It speaks to this idea that women are trying to assimilate more with the traditionally powerful gender, and those traits are being rewarded,” she says.Based on the team’s findings, Hoff says that business leaders and marketers could:Reexamine the traits their workplace rewardsIf companies want employees to feel comfortable expressing themselves—including through gender-fluid clothing—the research suggests their leaders must look at how they define success. In one of Hoff’s studies, participants were more likely to dress fluidly at workplaces where firms emphasized more traditionally “feminine” traits like collaboration and empathy over assertiveness and competition. “That would be a workplace context where men would also be more open to dressing more fluidly,” Hoff says.Reduce perceived risk around inclusive productsFor fashion brands, consumer adoption depends in part on whether gender-fluid styles feel socially acceptable. Hoff says brands could consider advertising that encourages consumers to “be bold and take risks,” and suggests featuring more men in gender-fluid clothing to help destigmatize those choices.Recognize that it takes more awareness to dismantle hierarchyThe research shows that merely selling inclusive products can still reinforce traditional power dynamics if uptake flows primarily toward masculine-coded signals.Understand the gender associations of all productsProducts from drinks to sports cars carry strong gender connotations that companies can challenge or reinforce, deliberately or unintentionally. If women are always bridging that divide, entrenched power systems are unlikely to change.“Gender associations are so important,” Hoff says. “The implications are broader than the context that we study.”

title: Photo of the day: Cutting-edge police tech on display in Delhi
description: A visitor looks at a Russian Kalashnikov drone displayed at the International Police and Drone Expo 2026 in New Delhi on June 24, 2026. Held at Bharat Mandapam in New Delhi, the event showcased cutting-edge law enforcement and homeland security technologies.Photo by Arun Sankar / AFP

title: Why Bhumi Pednekkar and Tamannaah Bhatia are more than just movie stars
description: Bhumi Pednekkar and Tamannaah Bhatia both admit they didn’t have much of a choice while starting out in movies. It was a matter of grabbing every opportunity they got, especially since they did not belong to film families.“Yes, I gained weight. But if someone would have asked me to chop off a finger, I would have done that too,” says Pednekkar, who put on 24 kg for her debut film, Dum Laga Ke Haisha (2015). Bhatia, on the other hand, made an unusual move by venturing down South early in her career, starring in Telugu and Tamil films although she was not familiar with the two languages. “For someone who is not from the film industry or does not have any connections, the only way is to see what you are getting and make that the best opportunity,” says Bhatia, who starred in the pan-India box office sensation Baahubali (2015).The two actors shared the stage at the Forbes India W-Power Self-Made Women event held in Mumbai on May 20, and spoke about creating a distinct identity for themselves in the entertainment world, navigating the challenges that come with stardom and their entrepreneurial experiences.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026“When I started my career, for me, success was also survival. It still is. I am lucky that I get to do something that I love,” says Pednekkar, who completed a decade in the industry last year. “Following a formula or a path that has been walked on is easy. Doing something that hasn’t been done scares people. And especially if you are a woman who’s walking your own path, you will scare society. They will constantly try to push you into a corner, they will try to box you.”Concurs Bhatia, who has had a two-decade journey in films already. “Success was not something that came immediately. But I knew I am going to enjoy doing this every day. And I just kept doing it, and there was one day when it worked,” she says.“I guess there are different pressures on actors who are from the film industry. But the difference is that I did not get a chance to prep. My prep was on location, on a film set. I had to learn two languages, Tamil and Telugu. I took the long road. I never took short cuts. But that helped me sustain.”Success also brought with it intense scrutiny and pressure. The two actors have been body-shamed at various times in their careers. They concede it is tough to deal with such trolling but have now learnt to stay immune to it.“It’s not about being actors… women are constantly picked on, commented on, because you are never meeting a beauty standard that has been set by men. Now, I really don’t care. I cannot let objectification or negativity affect me,” says Pednekkar, emphasising that public figures, in particular, are dehumanised. “The fact remains that anybody’s opinion about anybody is basically a reflection of them, and not a reflection of who I am,” points out Bhatia, who has chartbusters such as ‘Aaj ki Raat’ and ‘Ghafoor’ to her name.Though not a trained dancer, Bhatia fell in love with it over the years. She sees dance performances on screen as a celebration of women in their skin. “In fact, people forget movies, but they don’t forget songs. They are powerful and I will continue doing them,” she says.If dance is Bhatia’s passion, Pednekkar has been a climate crusader from a young age. She was named one of World Economic Forum’s Young Global Leaders a couple of years ago and has also invested in startups providing sustainable solutions.“I grew up with climate anxiety. I did not know I had it because mental health wasn’t ever discussed or spoken about back then,” she reveals. “I believed, as a child, that the world is going to reach a catastrophic point and it has. What felt like a fictional situation then is a reality we are living today. And it’s only going to keep getting worse. We have to ensure that we fight for our basic rights—clean air, clean nutrition, clean water.”Also Read: Tamannaah Bhatia's affair to rememberIn 2025, Pednekkar launched Backbay, a self-funded, premium beverage brand. She concedes she has probably entered the toughest category within the FMCG world. “It is one of the riskiest things I have done, but also one of the most gratifying ones,” she says, adding that every day as a founder is a new challenge.Bhatia, who turned entrepreneur with Tamannaah Fine Jewellery in January, believes once you become a businessperson you have to face every single fear of your life all the time. “I wanted to build an Indian brand that has a strong design language, and gives you style and volume. The retail business is what I want to work on and hopefully have it all over the country and globally too,” she says. “It’s important to ensure that your business makes money.”The two stars believe in the power of performance and hope to continue breaking boundaries along the way.

title: At over 50%, Russia’s share in India’s oil imports hits a record high in June
description: Russia accounts for more than half of India’s crude oil imports in June, surging to its highest share as cheap discounted barrels have displaced traditional Gulf suppliers against the backdrop of the war in West Asia.Russia’s share of India’s crude imports climbed to 52.5 percent in the first 22 days of June, up from 41.7 percent in May, according to an analysis of data from LSEG, a financial data provider.How India’s crude oil suppliers changedRussia's June figures are also a significant leap from the 22.4 percent share seen at the start of the year. The gains have come at the expense of India’s traditional Gulf suppliers. Saudi Arabia, for instance, has seen its share collapse to just 4.2 percent in June from nearly 21 percent in February, just before the war in West Asia broke out. Kuwait, Qatar and Iraq, which together accounted for over 30 percent of India’s imports in January, have effectively dropped to zero in June, shows LSEG data.Another notable addition in India’s crude oil suppliers this year is Venezuela. After US-imposed sanctions since 2019 kept Venezuelan barrels off India’s books, the South American producer has re-emerged as a supplier, capturing a 4.6 percent share in June. Though Venezuela’s June share is lower than those recorded in April and May, when they exceeded 5 percent, it has emerged as the third largest crude oil supplier to India. Before the US sanctions were imposed, Venezuela accounted for roughly 5-7 percent of India’s total crude oil imports.Also Read: How have commodity prices moved as Iran and the US begin talks in Europe?Effect of the West Asia warThe conflict in West Asia has been a key driver of the shift in India’s crude oil import profile. Its outbreak on February 28 injected uncertainty into transit via the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world’s seaborne oil passes. Before the beginning of the war, India used to get roughly 2.1 million barrels of crude per day through the Strait, making it the fourth-largest destination after China, South Korea and Japan.As the Iran-US standoff over the Strait of Hormuz intensified, it nudged Indian refiners to diversify away from Gulf-dependent supply chains.But that could change if the US-Iran peace agreement falls into place, paving the way for Iranian crude to re-enter global markets and easing concerns over the safe passage of ships through the Strait of Hormuz. An estimated 93 million barrels of non-Iranian crude remain stranded inside the Persian Gulf, according to shipping analytics firm Kpler. Saudi Arabia accounts for the largest share at 42.5 million barrels, followed by UAE-origin crude at 18.4 million, Kuwait and Qatar at 17.1 million, and Iraq at 15 million barrels. A further 72 million barrels of Iranian crude, held west of Chabahar, could add to a global supply once the Strait is fully reopened.India’s total crude imports through June 22 stood at 16.3 million metric tonnes. Crude imports for May were 2 percent higher than the same period last year. This was a significant rebound after imports declined 13 percent in March year-on-year when the war broke out in West Asia.

title: Why Indian consumers can’t yet connect to satellite internet
description: In March 2025, Airtel signed an agreement with Elon Musk’s SpaceX to explore bringing Starlink, its satellite internet service, to India. Reliance Jio announced a similar agreement with SpaceX a day later.Eutelsat OneWeb, in which the Bharti Group is a major investor, operates another satellite constellation and has already secured key approvals to provide satellite communication services in the country. Jio, too, has partnered with Luxembourg-based SES through JioSpaceFiber and is evaluating the development of a sovereign low-earth orbit satellite constellation.Yet, Indian consumers still cannot buy satellite broadband services at scale as spectrum rules, security clearances, pricing mechanisms, and regulatory frameworks are still being put in place by the government.On June 17, the Department of Telecommunications (DoT) released draft rules governing the administrative assignment of spectrum under the Telecommunications Act, 2023. However, the proposed framework stopped short of laying out a complete operating framework for satellite broadband providers.On June 24, the government released the Authorisation Rules, 2026, replacing India's licensing regime with a new authorisation-based system for telecom services.For satellite operators, the developments over the last two weeks are relevant because they help define the framework under which telecom and satellite services will operate. "If I speak broadly about satellite communications, this is the first time India has taken a clear policy path," says Narendra Singh, founder and CEO of investment research firm Growth Investing.At the same time, the announcements do not immediately translate into commercial services for consumers. Questions around implementation, operating conditions and commercial rollout remain under discussion.For now, that means that while satellite internet remains a technology that is generating significant interest, it is yet to become widely available to consumers in India.What is satellite internet?In India, people access the internet through mobile towers or fibre-optic cables. When a user streams a video, sends a WhatsApp message or joins a video call, the data typically travels through terrestrial infrastructure such as mobile towers, fibre networks and data centres.For satellite internet, instead of relying entirely on infrastructure built on the ground, data is transmitted through satellites orbiting the Earth. Users connect through specialised terminals that communicate with satellites overhead, which then relay information to ground stations connected to the wider internet.While satellite internet has dominated telecom discussions over the past year, the technology itself and the demand for it are not new. "I wouldn't call it sudden, the need [for satellite internet] has always been there," says Anku Jain, managing director of MediaTek India. "What has genuinely changed in the last 18 months is the shift from geostationary (GEO) to low-earth orbit (LEO) satellites."Unlike traditional GEO satellites positioned much farther from Earth, LEO satellites orbit much closer to the planet, reducing latency and improving performance. The shift has made satellite internet a more realistic option for broadband connectivity, particularly in locations where laying fibre or building telecom towers is difficult or uneconomical.Why is there interest in satellite internet?India had more than 1.09 billion internet subscribers as of March 2026, according to data released by the Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (TRAI). Of these, around 652 million were in urban areas, while about 441 million were in rural India.Yet, coverage challenges remain in remote locations where laying fibre or building telecom towers can be difficult. "The biggest challenge it addresses is coverage in locations where deploying terrestrial infrastructure is difficult due to geography, population density, or economic viability," Jain says.Industry executives do not view satellite internet as a replacement for fibre broadband or 5G networks. "Honestly, I do not see satellite as a competitor to telecom. The two are complementary," says Singh of Growth Investing. "Satellite will never replace fibre or 5G in urban India because terrestrial networks remain significantly cheaper and more efficient."Instead, satellite connectivity is expected to be used in locations where conventional infrastructure is difficult to deploy, as well as for applications such as maritime connectivity, disaster response and remote enterprise operations.Industry experts point to a combination of regulatory and operational hurdles that must be overcome before consumers can access satellite internet. "The bottleneck isn't any one thing. It is the sequential nature of all of them together," Jain says. Operators require spectrum, security clearances, gateway infrastructure, operating approvals and compliance with regulatory requirements before services can be launched commercially."The biggest bottleneck remains regulatory and operational readiness rather than technology," says Vinish Bawa, partner and telecom sector leader at PwC India. "Spectrum assignment frameworks, security compliance requirements, gateway approvals, coordination with multiple agencies, and clarity on commercial operating conditions all need to be finalised before services can scale."Another issue is the cost of the service. While operators are yet to announce final retail tariffs for India, satellite broadband may be more expensive than conventional broadband services. In late 2025, Starlink's India website briefly displayed a monthly subscription price of Rs8,600 and hardware costs of around Rs34,000 before the company withdrew the figures, saying they were test data and not final India pricing.When could consumers get satellite internet?While neither the government nor operators have announced a launch timeline, industry executives expect the first services to arrive before satellite broadband becomes mainstream.Jain expects initial commercial launches and enterprise deployments over the next six to 12 months, subject to final regulatory clearances and spectrum assignment. However, he cautions that consumer adoption will take longer as operators build distribution networks, install gateways and reduce equipment costs.Meanwhile, Singh expects pilot deployments to begin in 2026 and enterprise adoption to scale in 2027. Meaningful consumer adoption, he says, is more likely to emerge around 2028, with mass-market adoption taking place later in the decade."The government wants assurance that national interests will be protected. The companies want policy certainty and speed," Singh says.

title: EngageX 2026: Spotlighting customer experience in the era of AI
description: As artificial intelligence reshapes customer interactions across industries, business leaders are racing to explore how organizations can harness technology without losing the trust that underpins customer relationships.This concern sat at the heart of Exotel EngageX 2026, presented by Forbes India and CNBC-TV18. Bringing together leaders from banking, insurance, aviation, logistics, retail, technology, and digital-native businesses, the event explored how customer experience is evolving from a support function into a strategic driver of growth, loyalty, and enterprise value.The evening opened with a discussion aptly titled Trust is Not a Brand Attribute, It's an Economic Asset. Moderated by CNBC-TV18's Latha Venkatesh, the conversation brought together leaders from the BFSI sector and Exotel, the leader in AI-led customer experience solutions and cloud communications for enterprises.During the discussion, Shivakumar Ganesan, Co-founder and CEO of Exotel, emphasized, "Technology certainly helps, but ultimately, it must help humans." Tarun Chugh, MD & CEO of Bajaj Life, described trust as a "ledger" built through every customer interaction, while Dhiraj Relli of HDFC Securities argued that reliability and availability remain the foundations of trust in a digital-first world. Alok Aggarwal of Muthoot Homefin distilled trust-building into a simple formula: commit, deliver, and repeat consistently.The focus then shifted to the future of intelligent customer experiences in Building CX That Learns, Predicts and Compounds. The panel explored how organizations are moving from reactive customer service to predictive engagement. Vijaybahu Joshi of Reliance Jio argued that enterprises often overlook regional and cultural nuances in customer behavior, while Marina Datta of Birla Opus stressed the need for hyper-personalization built on data. Swiggy's Rohan Chhazed highlighted how customers increasingly expect brands to anticipate their needs before they articulate them.One of the recurring themes throughout the day was that customer experience itself is becoming a key differentiator. That idea came through strongly during The Experience P&L Panel, where leaders from DTDC, Zepto and Exotel debated how CX should be measured. Retention emerged as the dominant metric. DTDC CEO Abhishek Chakraborty noted that every year begins with a clean slate regardless of past success, while Zepto's Divesh Sawhney described repeat transactions as the ultimate validation of customer loyalty. Exotel's Sachin Bhatia pointed to customer effort score as a leading indicator of future retention, reflecting growing consumer impatience and rising expectations.Perhaps the most talked-about moment of the evening came when Shivakumar Ganesan demonstrated what Exotel described as India's first autonomous AI call. Unlike conventional AI-assisted interactions that require human orchestration, the demonstration featured an AI agent independently initiating conversations, gathering information, and scheduling follow-ups. The live showcase offered a glimpse into a future where AI agents may increasingly act on behalf of both businesses and customers.The event also featured a series of fireside conversations that highlighted how customer experience transformation is unfolding across industries. In a conversation titled When Business Becomes a Best Friend, Rajesh Dogra, Chief Customer Experience Officer at Air India, discussed the airline's ongoing transformation journey following its return to the Tata Group. He revealed that Air India has worked to reimagine more than 37 customer touchpoints across the travel journey, from booking to baggage collection.The BFSI sector, where trust remains paramount, featured prominently throughout the evening. Girish Sehgal of ICICI Lombard outlined how AI is helping accelerate claims processing, strengthen fraud detection, and improve underwriting precision. Vijay Rajagopal of AWS argued that India's digital public infrastructure and vast transaction volumes provide an unparalleled testing ground for AI innovation at scale.Trust and transformation also formed the backbone of discussions with banking leaders. Rama Mohan Rao Amara, Managing Director – Retail Business & Operations at SBI, spoke about the institution's enduring commitment to customer centricity while calling for greater agility within regulated environments. Meanwhile, IDFC FIRST Bank's V. Vaidyanathan offered a compelling reflection on trust during times of crisis, recounting the bank's swift response to a high-profile fraud incident and emphasizing that public confidence is earned through decisive and transparent action.The evening concluded with the unveiling of the cover of a book titled Architects of CX, Exotel's annual initiative that brings together the voices behind the best in customer experience. The book showcases the stories behind the scenes: the challenges these leaders navigated, the opportunities they spotted, and how they led the transformations that make AHA CX possible.The most striking takeaway from EngageX 2026 was that while AI may redefine how organizations interact with customers, trust remains the foundation upon which every meaningful customer relationship is built. At the end of the day, the tools may change, but the principles endure.To know more about Exotel, log on to https://exotel.com/Click here to watch more content: https://ms.forbesindia.com/exotel-engagex/The pages slugged ‘Brand Connect’ are equivalent to advertisements and are not written and produced by Forbes India journalists.

title: Pragya Misra is building India's AI bridge
description: When Pragya Misra joined OpenAI as its first hire in India, there was no local entity, no office and no roadmap. But that didn’t bother her. “That was precisely the opportunity,” she says. “We had a chance to help define how one of the world’s most important AI markets would engage with this technology.”It wasn’t the first time she’d walked into a room that didn’t yet exist. She was among WhatsApp’s earliest hires in India, building its communications and advocacy infrastructure. At Truecaller, she led public affairs as the company navigated the complicated terrain of identity, privacy and trust at scale. Each role required the same core skill: Translating something technically significant into something politically and publicly legible.At OpenAI, that skill is now operating at a different order of magnitude. ‘The early work was about listening,” she says. “I spent time with developers, researchers, startups, educators, civil society organisations, business leaders and policymakers to understand where AI could create the most value and what responsible adoption would look like in India.”W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026That groundwork appears to have paid off. India is now OpenAI’s second-largest market by user base, with adoption growing more than threefold year over year. But what Misra finds more telling is where the growth is coming from. “Students, creators, educators, professionals, founders, non-profits and public institutions are all finding practical ways to use AI in their daily work,” she says. “That’s the clearest signal of a technology reaching an inflection point. It stops being something people experiment with and becomes something they rely on.”While Misra’s career—from EY to co-founding an IT company, to WhatsApp, to Truecaller, to OpenAI—looks eclectic on paper, she disagrees it is so. “There has always been a consistent thread,” she says. “I’ve always been drawn to moments when technology changes how society functions at scale.”The qualities that have carried her across those transitions were visible early. “When Pragya joined the team, she distinguished herself through her drive, focus and commitment to excellence. She consistently took ownership, delivered strong results, and built trust through effective collaboration with colleagues and clients,” says Rajiv Memani, chairman and CEO of EY India and managing partner of the Africa-India region, who worked with her between 2013 and 2014. “These qualities have fuelled her growth and continue to define her leadership today.”What the breadth has given her, she argues, is pattern recognition. “The diversity of my career has made me a generalist. It helps me connect dots, pattern-match across industries, and bring different perspectives together.”The launch of ChatGPT Go at ₹399 a month—a significant pricing concession for India—is one visible result of her work. “I don’t see accessibility and sustainability as opposing goals,” Misra says. “The most transformative technologies become sustainable because they reach large numbers of people and create meaningful value in their lives.”Also Read: Dr Sivaranjani Santosh's prescription for changeFor India specifically, she argues, the equalising potential of AI goes beyond price. Its multimodal and multilingual capabilities, she says, can help bridge barriers of language, literacy and access, bringing intelligence closer to those who have historically been left out of the digital economy. “When more people have access to intelligence, knowledge and capability, more people can learn, innovate, create and participate in economicopportunity.”Away from work, Misra is a former national-level amateur golfer, a meditation trainer and the host of Pragyaan, a podcast about ideas, decisions and what gives life meaning.Golf taught her that the game is won internally while meditation ingrained in her that clarity comes from stillness.Both feel relevant to the role she now occupies—shaping AI policy for a billion-plus people at a moment when most of them are forming their first impressions of what AI will do to their lives. “The challenge isn’t simply the pace of technological progress,” she says. “It’s ensuring that understanding, access and opportunity keep pace with it. If AI only benefits a small group of people, we will have missed one of the greatest opportunities of our generation.”What gives her optimism is India’s track record. The country has absorbed mobile internet, digital payments and digital public infrastructure at a scale and speed that surprised the world each time. She believes AI will follow the same arc—but with India doing more than adopting, and help defining it.“As technology transforms how we work, live and relate to one another, what will matter most is not just intelligence, but wisdom,” she says. “Machines may increasingly tell us what we can do. But the deeper question of whether we should do it, and why, will always remain profoundly human.”

title: Dr Sivaranjani Santosh's prescription for change
description: For years, Dr Sivaranjani Santosh’s work was not about court petitions, regulatory orders or social media campaigns. It was about helping parents avoid needing a doctor in the first place.A paediatrician based in Hyderabad, Sivaranjani built her practice around prevention. She taught parents first-aid and CPR, counselled mothers on breastfeeding and infant care, and spent years trying to equip families with the knowledge to make informed decisions about their children’s health. “My aim was always to empower parents with the right knowledge and parenting skills,” she says. “So they are capable of taking care of their children in the best possible way and reach out to us only when need be.”That philosophy would eventually lead her into an eight-year battle that brought her national attention and contributed to a significant change in the way sugary drinks are labelled in India.Medicine was never a backup plan. Although her family discouraged her from pursuing it because they believed the profession was too demanding, Sivaranjani says she never seriously considered another career. “From when I was a child, it was fixed in my head that I’ll become a doctor.”After studying at JIPMER in Puducherry and specialising in paediatrics, she worked in different clinical settings before settling in Hyderabad. At Birthplace Hospital, she introduced prenatal classes for expectant mothers and practices aimed at encouraging breastfeeding from the earliest possible moments after birth. Later, she began conducting first-aid and CPR training sessions for parents.W-Power List 2026 | Meet India's Top Self-Made Women of 2026The issue that would define the next chapter of her career emerged unexpectedly when a mother asked Sivaranjani whether a popular packaged drink was the same as oral rehydration solution (ORS). She answered instinctively that it was. Later, when she started seeing children with prolonged diarrhoea and worsening dehydration, she began asking parents to bring the drinks they were giving their children.What she found was very concerning. As she looked closely at the packaging of drinks being sold as ORS, she found that pharmacies were misleading customers by selling them drinks that did not conform to the World Health Organization’s recommended ORS formulation. What followed was years of letters to regulators, conversations with medical bodies, social media campaigns, and, eventually, a public interest litigation.Finally, in October 2025, the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) said that products not meeting WHO-recommended standards could not use the term ORS on their labels. “Then I cried and cried and cried,” she recalls the moment the order was passed. “It was such a relief for me.”The fight, however, was never simply about a product label. For Sivaranjani, it represented a larger question about public health, ethics and the responsibility of professionals to speak up against something wrong.Also Read: Sonali Ghosh: The keeper of KazirangaThe campaign also came at personal and professional costs. She faced criticism from sections of the medical community, resistance from industry stakeholders and legal notices challenging her public statements.Yet, colleagues say, persistence has long been one of her defining traits. Dr Chetan Ginigeri, director of paediatrics at Manipal Hospitals, Bengaluru, has known Sivaranjani for nearly three decades, dating back to their days as classmates in medical school. “She always stood for what was right,” he says. “She was never somebody who would shy away from expressing what she believed was right for the child and the family.”According to Ginigeri, people may have become aware of Sivaranjani because of the ORS campaign, but the work behind it was years in the making. He points to her efforts in community education, injury prevention and CPR training as examples of a commitment that extends beyond the consulting room.That commitment remains central to how Sivaranjani sees her role. Even after the regulatory orders, she continues to focus on educating parents and raising awareness about child health issues. In her view, the larger challenge remains ensuring that parents understand what they are buying and that products are marketed in ways that do not create confusion.She continues to engage with regulators, raise awareness on social media and track developments in the category. The work, she says, is less about a single product than about protecting the integrity of public health information. “I have to take this to its logical conclusion,” she says.Sivaranjani’s journey has been nothing short of extraordinary. The doctor who once taught mothers what to expect in the first days after childbirth now finds herself speaking to audiences far beyond hospital walls.

title: Graphic of the day: Stranded crude in the Gulf
description: An estimated 93 million barrels of non-Iranian crude remain stranded inside the Persian Gulf, according to shipping analytics firm Kpler. Saudi Arabia accounts for the largest share at 42.5 million barrels, followed by UAE-origin crude at 18.4 million, Kuwait and Qatar at 17.1 million, and Iraq at 15 million barrels. A further 72 million barrels of Iranian crude, held west of Chabahar, could add to a global supply glut once the Strait of Hormuz reopens.

title: Sonali Ghosh: The keeper of Kaziranga
description: At 4.30 am in the wild pockets of Assam, much before sunlight has hit the thick elephant grass, Sonali Ghosh is at work.The animal kingdom has begun to stir, and by 5.15 am, the first set of tourists will enter the Kaziranga National Park for a unique safari experience: Riding aboard trained elephants for a majestic vantage point, which lets you get up close with the famous one-horned rhinoceros.Assam is home to about 80 percent of the world’s population of the greater one-horned rhino and in the last year, a record 5 lakh tourists came to Kaziranga to see them. This included 30,000 international tourists, a 100 percent jump over the previous year.Ghosh, who made history in 2023 when she was appointed the national park’s first female field director, manages not only the hospitality and tourist experience, but also the annual floods, threats of poaching and the challenging ecological habitat, along with engaging the local community in conservation efforts.
